frameworks.name: divisive normalization
frameworks.one_line: value coding scheme where each option's value is normalized by set-wide value

frameworks.name: rational choice theory
frameworks.one_line: baseline assuming context-independent preferences over alternatives

core_question: Do social choices (hiring, voting) exhibit context-dependent preferences, and which value-coding model best characterizes them?
key_assumptions: value coding schemes from perceptual/economic choice generalize to social evaluation
candidate competence can serve as a scalar value input to choice models
manipulations: distractor value (high- vs low-value third candidate) in hiring scenarios
domain: hypothetical hiring decisions vs archival U.S. congressional races
model comparison across alternative value-coding schemes
key_findings: divisive normalization best fit choice patterns in hiring studies (1a, 1b, 1d)
with participant random effects, higher-value distractors INCREASED choice of the best candidate — opposite of normalization's prediction
in congressional elections, candidate competence effects aligned with divisive normalization
interpretations: mixed support for a neurobiologically-derived normalization account of social choice
high-value distractors can either help or hurt relative choice accuracy depending on condition
limitations_noted: distractor effect direction reversed once participant random effects were modeled
